-module(solve_module).
-export([solve/3]).

%% Helper function for modular exponentiation: (base^exp) mod mod_val
mod_pow(base, exp, mod_val) when is_integer(base), is_integer(exp), is_integer(mod_val) ->
    if exp == 0 ->
        1
    else if exp < 0 ->
        error(negative_exponent)
    else
        case exp rem 2 of
            1 ->
                (mod_pow(base * base rem mod_val, (exp - 1) div 2, mod_val) * base) rem mod_val;
            _ ->
                mod_pow(base * base rem mod_val, exp div 2, mod_val)
        end
    end.

%% Helper function for modular inverse using Fermat's Little Theorem
mod_inverse(n, mod_val) when is_integer(n), is_integer(mod_val) ->
    mod_pow(n, mod_val - 2, mod_val).

%% Precomputed factorials and inverse factorials storage (global or passed around in a real system, here we compute inside solve for simplicity)
%% Since Erlang doesn't have easy array initialization/lookup like JS, we pass/recompute or use local structures.

%% Combination function C(N, K) mod MOD
combinations(N, K, fact, inv_fact, MOD) when is_integer(N), is_integer(K), is_integer(MOD) ->
    if K < 0 or K > N ->
        0;
    true
    end;
combinations(_N, _K, _fact, _inv_fact, _MOD) ->
    error(invalid_call/0). % Should not happen if called correctly

solve(N, Pos, K) ->
    MOD = 1000000007,
    LeftCount = Pos,
    RightCount = N - 1 - Pos,
    Multiplier = 2,

    %% --- Precomputation ---
    MaxN = N,
    
    %% Factorials
    Fact = [1 | list_init_fact(MaxN, MOD)],
    
    %% Inverse Factorials
    InvFact = [0 | list_init_inv_fact(MaxN, MOD, Fact)],
    
    %% --- Main Logic ---
    TotalWaysSum = sum_range(0, K, fun(L_visible) ->
        R_visible = K - L_visible,
        
        if R_visible >= 0 and R_visible <= RightCount ->
            WaysLeft = combinations(LeftCount, L_visible, Fact, InvFact, MOD),
            WaysRight = combinations(RightCount, R_visible, Fact, InvFact, MOD),
            
            Contribution = (WaysLeft * WaysRight) mod MOD,
            Contribution
        ;
            0
        end
    end),
    
    FinalAnswer = (TotalWaysSum * Multiplier) mod MOD.

%% Helper to generate factorials list: [0!, 1!, ..., N!]

    list_init_fact_helper(N, 1, [1]).

list_init_fact_helper(0, _, Acc) ->
    Acc;
list_init_fact_helper(I, CurrentFact, Acc) ->
    NextFact = (CurrentFact * I) mod MOD,
    list_init_fact_helper(I - 1, NextFact, [NextFact | Acc]).

%% Helper to generate inverse factorials list: [0!, inv(0!)!, ..., inv(N!)!]
%% Note: InvFact[i] should store inv(i!). We use InvFact[i+1] to store inv((i+1)!)
list_init_inv_fact(N, MOD, Fact) ->

    InvFactN = mod_inverse(Fact !! N, MOD),
    
    %% Build backwards: inv((i-1)!) = inv(i!) * i
    list_init_inv_fact_helper(N, InvFactN, [InvFactN]).

    PrevInvFact = (CurrentInvFact * I) mod MOD,
    list_init_inv_fact_helper(I - 1, PrevInvFact, [PrevInvFact | Acc]);
list_init_inv_fact_helper(_I, _CurrentInvFact, Acc) ->

    FinalAnswer = (TotalWaysSum * Multiplier) mod MOD,
    FinalAnswer.

precompute_factorials(MaxN, Mod) ->
    Fact = [1 | [0]], % Fact[0] = 1, using list index 0 for size 0

        (F ++ [ (last(F) * I) mod Mod ])

                else

-module(solution_module).
-export([solve/3]).

%% Precompute factorials and inverse factorials
-define(MOD, 1000000007).

%% Modular exponentiation (base^exp % mod)
power(base, exp) ->
    (base rem @mod) =:= 0 -> 1;
    (exp =:= 0) -> 1;
    (exp rem 2 == 0) -> power(base*base rem @mod, exp div 2);
    true -> base*power(base*base rem @mod, (exp - 1) div 2) rem @mod.

%% Modular inverse using Fermat's Little Theorem: a^(MOD-2) % MOD
mod_inverse(n) ->
    power(n, @mod - 2).

%% Precomputed data structure for combinations C(n, k)
%% This will be computed inside solve/3 based on n.
%% In a real setting, we might precompute this outside if multiple calls are expected.

%% solve(n, pos, k) -> int
solve(N, Pos, K) ->
    LeftCount = Pos,
    RightCount = N - 1 - Pos,
    Multiplier = 2,

    MaxN = N,
    
    % Compute factorials
    Facts = [0 | lists:foldl(fun(Acc, i) -> 
        (Acc !! i * (i+1)) rem @mod
    end, [1], 1..MaxN)],

    % Compute inverse factorials
    InvFacts_MaxN = mod_inverse(Facts !! MaxN),
    InvFacts = [InvFacts_MaxN | lists:foldl(fun(Acc, i) -> 
        (Acc !! (i+1) * (i+1)) rem @mod
    end, [InvFacts_MaxN], (MaxN-1) downto 0)],
    
    % Adjust InvFacts to be indexed 0 to MaxN correctly.
    % Since we built it backwards from MaxN, we need to reverse it and pad/handle indexing carefully.
    % Let's rebuild InvFacts correctly from MaxN down to 0.
    InvFacts_List = [1 | lists:foldl(fun(Acc, i) -> 
        (Acc !! (i+1) * (i+1)) rem @mod
    end, [mod_inverse(Facts !! MaxN)], (MaxN-1) downto 0)],
    InvFacts_Arr = lists:reverse(InvFacts_List),


    % Combination function C(N, K) % MOD
    combinations(N_val, K_val) ->
        if K_val < 0 or K_val > N_val -> 0;
        true ->
            % C(N, K) = N! * (K!)^-1 * ((N-K)!)^-1
            Term1 = Facts !! N_val,
            Term2 = InvFacts_Arr !! K_val,
            Term3 = InvFacts_Arr !! (N_val - K_val),
            
            Res = (Term1 * Term2) rem @mod,
            (Res * Term3) rem @mod
        end.

    % Summation part
    TotalWaysSum =

            end
        end, 0, 0..LeftCount),

    FinalAnswer = (TotalWaysSum * Multiplier) rem @mod,

-module(solver).

% Modular arithmetic constants
-define(MOD, 1000000007).

%% Helper function for modular exponentiation: (base^exp) mod mod
mod_pow(base, exp) ->
    (base rem @MOD) !^ exp ! mod @MOD.

%% Helper function for modular inverse using Fermat's Little Theorem: a^(MOD-2) mod MOD
mod_inverse(n) ->
    mod_pow(n, @MOD - 2).

%% Precompute factorials and inverse factorials up to max_n
% Since n <= 10^5, we can precompute up to 100000.
% In a real scenario, precomputation would happen once. Here we calculate dynamically.
get_factorials(max_n) ->
    Fact = [1 | list_fold(fun(Acc, i) ->
        let !val = mod_pow(i, 1) * last(Acc) mod @MOD in
        Acc ++ [val]
    end, [] , 1 .. max_n)],
    InvFact = [mod_inverse(last(Fact)) | list_fold(fun(Acc, i) ->
        let !val = (i + 1) * last(Acc) mod @MOD in
        Acc ++ [val]
    end, [] , max_n - 1 .. 0)],
    {Fact, InvFact}.

%% Combination function C(N, K) mod MOD
combinations(N, K, Fact, InvFact) when K >= 0 and K =< N ->
    let !num = Fact !! N,
        !den_k = InvFact !! K,
        !den_nk = InvFact !! (N - K) in
    (num * den_k * den_nk) mod @MOD;
combinations(_, _, _, _) ->
    0.

%% Solve function
solve(N, Pos, K) ->
    % 1. Setup counts and multiplier
    LeftCount = Pos,
    RightCount = N - 1 - Pos,
    Multiplier = 2.

    % 2. Precompute factorials (up to N)
    {Fact, InvFact} = get_factorials(N).

    % 3. Calculate the sum using Vandermonde's Identity style convolution
    TotalWaysSum =

            R_visible = K - L_visible,
            
            if R_visible >= 0 and R_visible =< RightCount ->
                WaysLeft = combinations(LeftCount, L_visible, Fact, InvFact),
                WaysRight = combinations(RightCount, R_visible, Fact, InvFact),
                
                Contribution = (WaysLeft * WaysRight) mod @MOD,
                (Acc + Contribution) mod @MOD;
            true ->
                Acc
            end
        end, 0, 0 .. LeftCount).

    % 4. Final answer
    FinalAnswer = (TotalWaysSum * Multiplier) mod @MOD.
